revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八神殿]][[simple:Hasshinden]]
{{distinguish|{{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|座摩神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|2=ja|3=座摩神|4=zh|5=坐摩巫祭神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}}}
[[File:Jingikan_map.svg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} layout The northern office where the {{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|座摩神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|2=ja|3=座摩神|4=zh|5=坐摩巫祭神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} are enshrined is prominent in the northwest. The {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} is located just to the southwest and is the westernmost major building]] '''Hasshinden''' (八神殿) was a shrine dedicated to eight deities tasked with protecting the {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.<ref name=":1" />
During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the Hasshinden was located within the western courtyard of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} in the {{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} Imperial Palace. In later periods, after the decline of the palace's original Hasshinden, similar shrines were established within the precincts of {{ill|Yoshida Shrine|en|Yoshida Shrine|qid=Q713177|lt=Yoshida Shrine|1=Yoshida Shrine}} and the residence of the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白川伯王家}} during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}.<ref name=":0">https://www.amazon.ca/New-History-Shinto-John-Breen/dp/1405155167</ref>{{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=9-10}} Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the Hasshinden was relocated to the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|qid=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}} and integrated into the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}}.{{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=9-10}} Today, the deities are enshrined collectively in the {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|qid=Q44539|lt=Shinden|1=Temple}} at the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|qid=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}} as part of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}}.{{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=9-10}}<ref name=":1" />
The following eight deities are enshrined, as listed in the ''{{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engishiki|1=Engishiki}}'' and the ''{{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|qid=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi|1=Kogo Shūi}}'' with slightly different nomenclature but referring to the same entities:<ref name=":1" />
{| class="wikitable" style="background:#ffffff;" ! !! colspan="2" |''Engishiki'' Shrine Registry !! rowspan="2" | ''[[Kogo Shūi]] kanji'' |- ! Hall !! Name !Kanji |- | First Hall || '''{{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ|lt=Kami-musubi-no-kami}}''' |神産日神|| 神皇産霊神 |- | Second Hall || '''[[Takamimusubi|Takami-musubi-no-kami]]''' |高御産日神|| 高皇産霊神 |- | Third Hall || [[Amaterasu|'''Tama-tsume-musubi-no-kami''']] |玉積産日神|| 魂留産霊 |- | Fourth Hall || '''{{ill|Ame-no-Ikutama|qid=Q97184246|lt=Iku-musubi-no-Kami|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}}''' |生産日神|| 生産霊 |- | Fifth Hall || [[Ōkuninushi|'''Taru-musubi-no-kami''']] |足産日神|| 足産霊 |- | Sixth Hall || '''{{ill|Ōmiya-no-Me|ja|大宮売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ|lt=Ōmiya-no-Me-no-kami}}''' |大宮売神|| 大宮売神 |- | Seventh Hall || '''{{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Miketsu-no-Kami|ja|御食津神|1=Ukanomitama|lt=Miketsu-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}}''' |御食津神|| 御膳神 |- | Eighth Hall || '''{{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}''' |事代主神|| 事代主神 |}The {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} and {{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|座摩神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|2=ja|3=座摩神|4=zh|5=坐摩巫祭神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were both prominently worshipped in the Imperial palace. But the Hasshinden were seen as having a cosmological importance<ref>https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8587</ref> and the Ikasuri domestic.<ref>https://en.osaka-info.jp/spot/ikasurijinja/</ref>
With the enshrinement of many more deities in the Meiji period the Shinden became like the [[Sōja shrine]] for all of Japan.
It is theorized that {{nihongo|Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami|玉積産日神}} was [[Amaterasu]] and {{nihongo|Taru-musubi-no-kami|足産日神}} was [[Ōkuninushi]], until both were moved out of the Imperial palace during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].
== Overview == In the imperial court of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ({{ill|Heian-kyō|en|Heian-kyō|qid=Q751907|lt=Heian-kyō|1=Heian-kyō}} {{ill|Heian Palace|en|Heian Palace|qid=Q304501|lt=Heian Palace|1=Heian Palace}}), important deities were venerated by female priests known as “Mikannagi” ([[Wiktionary:御巫|御巫]]) in the western precincts of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}, specifically by two (later three) Ōmikanna, one Zama-miko, one Mikado-miko, and one Ikushima-miko {{Sfn|Mikanna (National History)|}}. The term “Hasshinden” refers collectively to the eight shrines where the deities were enshrined by the '''Ōmikannagi''' (大御巫) {{Sfn|Yashinden (National History)|}}. These eight deities are of vital importance to the Emperor’s well-being; in the {{ill|Engi-shiki Deity Name Register|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engi-shiki Deity Name Register|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}, they are listed at the head of all 3,132 shrines nationwide.
According to ancient maps, the Hasshinden originally consisted of eight separate shrines—each enshrining one deity—lined up north–south along the western wall of the Jingi-kan’s western precinct, facing east {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=13-14}}. Surrounding these eight shrines was a vermilion jade wall measuring 10 shaku north–south and 3 shaku east–west on three sides; no sacred objects were housed within the halls, and only sakaki trees were placed there {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=13-14}}. Three torii gates were erected in front of the first, fifth, and eighth shrines on the jade wall {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=13-14}}. Furthermore, according to the temporary priestly garment regulations for the transfer of deities in the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engi-shiki|1=Engishiki}}, the divine vestments consisted of four sets for male deities and four sets for female deities; once the Mikanna reached adulthood, with each rotation the shrines, garments, and furnishings were completely renewed {{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|2011th year |pp=192-194}}. The serving Ōmikanna (simply “Mikanna”) were chosen from maidens aged seven or older; at the time of the divination recorded in the {{ill|Rishu-kai|ja|令集解|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rishu-kai|1=Ryōshūge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11378321}} for the Jingi-kan, they were composed of two Wa-country miko, but by the time of the {{ill|Engishiki|en|Engishiki|qid=Q1342448|lt=Engi-shiki|1=Engishiki}} Deity Name Register, they had been reformed to three for the Emperor, the Empress, and the Crown Prince {{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|2011th year |pp=186-187}}.
== Enshrined Deities == The first five deities – {{ill|Kamimusubi|en|Kamimusubi|qid=Q3275901|qid=Q3275901|lt=Kamimusubi|1=Kamimusubi}}, {{ill|Takamimusubi|en|Takamimusubi|qid=Q125400|qid=Q125400|lt=Takamimusubi|1=Takamimusubi}}, {{nihongo|Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami|玉積産日神}}, {{nihongo|Iku-musubi-no-kami|生産日神}}, and {{nihongo|Taru-musubi-no-kami|足産日神}} – are collectively known as the “Musubi Deities,” associated with the soul. In particular, {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}} and {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産日神|1=Takamimusubi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}} are two of the three creative deities known as the {{ill|Kotoamatsukami|en|Kotoamatsukami|qid=Q776371|lt=Kotoamatsukami|1=Kotoamatsukami}}. The next deity, {{ill|Oomiya-no-me|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya-no-me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=}}, is regarded both as a personification of the palace and as the deified form of an {{ill|Naishi|ja|内侍|1=Naishi (court lady)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi|13=User:Immanuelle/Naishi (court lady)|qq=}} (court lady), serving as an intermediary between the ruler and his subjects {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=15-16}}{{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|2011th year |pp=186-187}}. Additionally, {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Miketsu-gami|ja|御食津神|1=Ukanomitama|lt=Miketsu-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ウカノミタマ}} governs food, and {{ill|Kotoshironushi|en|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} governs words (and is generally regarded as distinct from the Izumo branch of {{ill|Kotoshironushi|en|Kotoshironushi|qid=Q8009611|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}) {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=15-16}}{{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|2011th year |pp=186-187}}.
Furthermore, according to the {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|qid=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi|1=Kogo Shūi}}, during the reign of the first Emperor {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, a shrine ({{ill|Himorogi|en|Himorogi|qid=Q60587|lt=Himorogi|1=Himorogi}}) was built by imperial decree of the two heavenly ancestral deities ({{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} and {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}), enshrining {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高皇産霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神皇産霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami, Iku-musubi-no-kami, Taru-musubi-no-kami,, {{ill|Oomiya-no-me|ja|大宮売神|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya-no-me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to オオミヤノメ}}, {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}, and {{ill|Ukanomitama|lt=Miketsu-gami|ja|御膳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukanomitama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with the ritual beginning from that point {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|p=17}}.
It is noteworthy that among these eight enshrined deities—each directly connected to the Emperor—the {{ill|Ancestors of the Emperor|lt=Imperial Ancestor Deity|ja|皇祖神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancestors of the Emperor|qq=}} {{ill|Amaterasu|en|Amaterasu|qid=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|1=Amaterasu}} is not included, which is one of its distinctive features {{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|2011th year |pp=186-187}}. In ancient times, Amaterasu was never enshrined within the court; according to the tradition recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, she was exiled from the court during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} (the 10th Emperor) (she is now enshrined at {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}). The common theory is that Amaterasu was positioned as the supreme deity of the court only from the latter half of the 7th century, and that prior to this the highest deity was {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産日神|1=Takamimusubi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}<ref>Teruo Kikuchi, “Takamimusubi,” in {{ill|Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja|日本古代史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihon Kodai-shi Daijiten|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Yamato Shobo, 2006.</ref>. Another theory suggests that around the late 7th century, Takamimusubi was enshrined in the court while Amaterasu was allocated to Ise <ref>Hideichi Takezawa, {{ill|Solving the Mysteries of Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮の謎を解く|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Solving the Mysteries of Ise Grand Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Chikuma Shinsho, 2011, p. 116.</ref>.
== History == Regarding the eight deities, aside from their earlier description in the {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|qid=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi|1=Kogo Shūi}} compiled in the 2nd year of the {{ill|Daidō|en|Daidō|qid=Q1373191|lt=Tai-dō|1=Daidō}} (807), an account in the 3rd year of the {{ill|Ten'an|en|Ten'an|qid=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} (859) records that the Ranks ({{ill|Shinkai (divine rank)|en|Shinkai (divine rank)|qid=Q11591025|lt=Shinkai (divine rank)|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}}) of the five deities {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産日神|1=Takamimusubi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{nihongo|Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami|玉積産日神}}, {{nihongo|Iku-musubi-no-kami|生産日神}}, and {{nihongo|Taru-musubi-no-kami|足産日神}} advanced from “no Rank” to Juichii; four days later, an article notes that the Ranks of {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産日神|1=Takamimusubi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{nihongo|Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami|玉積産日神}}, and {{nihongo|Taru-musubi-no-kami|足産日神}} reached the highest Rank, Shōichii, as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}.
In the {{ill|Enchō|ja|延長|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Enchō|1=Enchō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q97379316}} 5th year (927), the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}}—established that year—recorded the deities as “Mikanna Eight Deities, in line with the New Rice Offering during Tsukimi,” and noted that during the {{ill|Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} and the {{ill|Niiname-no-Matsuri|en|Niiname-no-Matsuri|qid=Q11501518|lt=Niinamesai|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}, they were entrusted with offerings of heihaku {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=13-14}}.
=== Shirakawa Family === In 1161, control of the Jingikan was transferred to the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白川伯王家}}, a {{ill|Emperor Kazan|en|Emperor Kazan|qid=Q361000|lt=Kazan|1=Emperor Kazan}} [[Minamoto_clan|Genji]] clan, marking the start of their hereditary management over the institution. Under their authority, the Hasshinden—a hall dedicated to the eight deities charged with protecting the imperial family—was rebuilt in 1281, reinforcing its role in state rituals and the safeguarding of the imperial household. However, during the Onin War (1467–1477), the Shirakawa Hasshinden was destroyed amid the widespread turmoil and devastation that affected Kyoto.<ref>[https://archive.org/details/shintohistory0000hard Shinto : a history : Hardacre, Helen, 1949- : Free Download, Borrow, and Streaming : Internet Archive] Chapter 7</ref>
=== Yoshida family === Although the Jingi-kan’s ceremonies declined during the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=Medieval|1=Middle Ages}} period, the ancient form was maintained until the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|qid=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}}, but after their destruction in the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Onin War|1=Ōnin War}}, the court did not rebuild the shrines.{{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=9-10}}
The prompted [[Yoshida Kanetomo]] to reconstruct it as part of his grand religious complex, the ''Saijōsho'' (斎場所), on Mount Yoshida in Kyoto. The ''Saijōsho'' was intended to be a new sacred center for Japan, positioning Kanetomo as the supreme authority over Shinto affairs.<ref name=":0" />
In addition to his theories of Shinto, Kanemoto developed many rituals. He began by building the {{Nihongo|2=大元宮斎場所|3=Daigengū Saijōsho}} at Yoshida Shrine. This enshrined the ''kami'' of Ise Shrine, the Hasshinden, and the more than 3,000 ''kami'' of the Shikinaisha. He then declared the Daigengū to be the root of all religion in Japan from the time of its founding, as well as the main shrine for all shrines throughout the country.{{Sfn|Satoshi Ito|2012|pp=235–236}}
The Hasshinden was located at the rear of the ''Saijōsho'' courtyard, which also contained shrines dedicated to all of Japan’s kami. It stood alongside shrines dedicated to Amaterasu and Toyouke, the deities enshrined at Ise Grand Shrine’s Inner and Outer Shrines, respectively. The structure was part of Kanetomo's broader effort to redefine Shinto by asserting its supremacy over Buddhism and Confucianism, and by reclaiming the status of kami as independent divine beings rather than Buddhist manifestations (''honji suijaku'').<ref name=":0" />
Kanetomo’s revival of the Hasshinden was closely tied to his goal of centralizing Shinto under Yoshida control. The ''Saijōsho'', with the Hasshinden at its core, functioned as an alternative to the traditional Ise Grand Shrine, which Kanetomo sought to incorporate into his religious framework. He even claimed that sacred objects from Ise had miraculously appeared at the ''Saijōsho'', a claim that persuaded {{ill|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|en|Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|qid=Q380623|lt=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado|1=Emperor Go-Tsuchimikado}} (r. 1464–1500) to recognize the site’s legitimacy.<ref name=":0" />
Furthermore, Kanetomo introduced a hierarchical system of shrine certification, issuing licenses (''Shintō saikyōjo'') to Shinto priests and temples. The Hasshinden thus played a key role in the Yoshida family’s attempt to establish a centralized authority over Shinto practice, a system that was expanded by Kanetomo’s successor, {{ill|Yoshida Kanemigi|ja|吉田兼右|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshida Kanemigi|1=Yoshida Kanemigi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11413615}} (1516–1573).<ref name=":0" />
The Hasshinden, like the other structures in the ''Saijōsho'', reflected the unique theological principles of Yoshida Shinto. It was surrounded by a sacred courtyard and contained a central pillar of bamboo supporting a jewel, symbolizing divine presence. At eye level, an octagonal wooden plaque drew attention to the kami enshrined within. The architecture of the Hasshinden reinforced Kanetomo’s vision of Shinto as the primordial religion from which all other traditions derived.<ref name=":0" />
During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, new shrines dedicated to the eight deities were established at {{ill|Yoshida Shrine|en|Yoshida Shrine|qid=Q713177|lt=Yoshida Shrine|1=Yoshida Shrine}} and within the residence of the {{ill|Shirakawa family|ja|白川家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa family|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 白川伯王家}} as a substitute for the court’s Hasshinden.<ref name=":0" />{{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=9-10}} Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the Hasshinden was relocated to the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|qid=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}} and integrated into the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}}.{{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=9-10}}
=== Meiji period abolition === Following the Meiji Restoration, when the Jingi-kan was reestablished, a new shrine was constructed in the 2nd year of {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} (1869) and a relocation ceremony was held. At that time, in addition to the eight deities of the Hasshinden, the deities of {{ill|Tenshinjichi|ja|天神地祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsukami and Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|1=Kami#Amatsukami_and_Kunitsukami|lt=Amatsukami and Kunitsukami|2=ja|3=天津神・国津神|4=zh|5=天津神與國津神6=ko|7=아마쓰카미·구니쓰카미|lt_ja=天神地祇|lt=Tenjin Chigi}} and the ancestral spirits of successive Emperors were also enshrined. Until then, the ancestral spirits had been venerated in a Buddhist style at Kurodo, which was then abolished. In September of the 5th year of Meiji (1872), the Jingi-kan was reformed to conduct only religious teachings, and the Hasshinden was transferred from the Jingi-kan to the court, while the ancestral spirits were moved to the {{ill|Kōrei-den|ja|皇霊殿|lt=Kōrei-den (Imperial Ancestral Shrines)|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōrei-den|13=User:Immanuelle/Three Palace Sanctuaries|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宮中三殿}} of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}}. In October of the same year (1872), the eight deities of the Hasshinden were re-enshrined as part of the {{ill|Tenshinjichi|ja|天神地祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/tsukami and Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|1=Kami#Amatsukami_and_Kunitsukami|lt=Amatsukami and Kunitsukami|2=ja|3=天津神・国津神|4=zh|5=天津神與國津神6=ko|7=아마쓰카미·구니쓰카미|lt_ja=天神地祇|lt=Tenjin Chigi}}, the name “Hasshinden” was abolished, and the shrine was renamed simply “Shinden” (神殿). This shrine continues today as one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}} of the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|qid=Q500681|lt=Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}}.
In the Meiji era, the [[Department of Divinities]] constructed a shrine in Tokyo, and the sacred object was enshrined there on December 17, Meiji 2 (1862). {{Sfn|皇典講究所|1909|p=25-26}}
After the Jingi-kan was reorganized into the Ministry of Divinities—and that ministry was abolished in Meiji 5 (1872)—the eight gods and {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Amatsukami and Kunitsukami|ja|天神地祇|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amatsukami and Kunitsukami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天津神・国津神|1=Kami#Amatsukami_and_Kunitsukami|lt=Amatsukami and Kunitsukami|2=ja|3=天津神・国津神|4=zh|5=天津神與國津神6=ko|7=아마쓰카미·구니쓰카미}} were transferred to the Imperial Court. On November 27 of that same year they were enshrined together and the shrine was renamed as the Shin-den. {{Sfn|皇典講究所|1909|p=26-27}}<ref name=":1">https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8587</ref>
Every year, on the Vernal Equinox Day and the Autumnal Equinox Day, the spring and autumn Shin-den festivals are held.
=== Deity Ranks === * On January 27, {{ill|Ten'an|ja|天安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'an|1=Cheonan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3577288}} 3 (859), the five deities {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産日神|1=Takamimusubi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{nihongo|Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami|玉積産日神}}, {{nihongo|Iku-musubi-no-kami|生産日神}}, and {{nihongo|Taru-musubi-no-kami|足産日神}} advanced from “{{ill|Unranked|ja|无位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Unranked|qq=}}” to {{ill|Junior First Rank|ja|従一位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior First Rank|qq=}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}). * On February 1, {{ill|Ten'an|ja|天安|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ten'an|1=Cheonan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3577288}} 3 (859), they advanced from {{ill|Junior First Rank|ja|従一位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior First Rank|qq=}} to {{ill|Senior First Rank|ja|正一位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senior First Rank|qq=}} (as recorded in the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|en|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|qid=Q1052981|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}) – this time for {{ill|Kamimusubi|ja|神産日神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to カミムスビ}}, {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|高御産日神|1=Takamimusubi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamimusubi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to タカミムスビ}}, {{nihongo|Tamatsume-Musube-no-Kami|玉積産日神}}, and {{nihongo|Taru-musubi-no-kami|足産日神}}.
== Related Matters == [[File:Omiya-inari-jinja (Kyoto, Kyoto).JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Oomiya Hime Inari Shrine|ja|大宮姫稲荷神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya Hime Inari Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}})]] [[File:Omiyame-jinja, haiden.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Oomiya Shrine|ja|大宮売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya Shrine|1=Ōmiya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434413}} ({{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} {{ill|Kyōtango|en|Kyōtango|qid=Q513897|lt=Kyotango City|1=Kyōtango}})]]
The ancient ceremonies of the Jingi-kan had been completely abolished by the time of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Onin War|1=Ōnin War}}. The rites for the eight deities were revived during the Edo period, and since the Meiji era they have been transmitted in the shrine (one of the {{ill|Three Palace Sanctuaries|en|Three Palace Sanctuaries|qid=Q7797685|lt=Three Palace Sanctuaries|1=Three Palace Sanctuaries}}) of the court, although the content of the ceremonies has changed completely from ancient times {{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|pp=199-201}}.
As a shrine that preserves the tradition of the eight deities, the Hatsumi Shrine ({{ill|Hatsumi Myojin|ja|初宮明神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hatsumi Myojin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, located in Nabeyamachi, Nara City, Nara Prefecture) is noted. This shrine is a branch of the ancient Jingi-kan’s Hasshinden that once existed in the imperial court at {{ill|Heijō Palace|en|Heijō Palace|qid=Q211585|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=Heijō Palace}} during the Nara period. Similar to the Hasshinden established at {{ill|Yoshida Shrine|en|Yoshida Shrine|qid=Q713177|lt=Yoshida Shrine|1=Yoshida Shrine}} in the Edo period, it is closely associated with {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga Taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}} (Nara City, Nara Prefecture); at the Kasuga Wakamiya On-matsuri, Dengaku priests traditionally first offered Dengaku at Hatsumi Shrine {{Sfn|Nara City History: Shrines and Temples|pp=222}}.
Furthermore, among the eight deities, particularly {{ill|Oomiya-no-me|ja|オオミヤノメ|1=Ōmiya-no-Me|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya-no-me|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya-no-Me|qq=}}, is venerated in Kyoto as the Oomiya Hime Inari Shrine (located in Shuzai-chō, Kamigyō Ward, Kyoto City, Kyoto Prefecture) {{Sfn|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)|pp=21-23}}. Also, as a related shrine, the only in-ceremony shrine known by the name “Oomiya” is the {{ill|Oomiya Shrine|ja|大宮売神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya Shrine|1=Ōmiya Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11434413}} (in Kyotango City, Kyoto Prefecture) {{Sfn|Ancient History of Shrines|pp=186-187}}.
In connection with the Jingi-kan’s Hasshinden, it is also known that during the ancient Daijosai temporary structures called “Hasshinden” were erected at the Saijōin of Kyoto and at the ritual sites of the two countries of Yūki and Shuki. In these, the following deities were enshrined: :* {{ill|Toshigami|ja|御歳神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 年神}} :* {{ill|Takamitama-no-kami|ja|高御魂神|lt=Takamitama-no-kami|lt_ja=高御魂神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takamitama-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :* Niwa Takahiko (庭高日神) :* {{ill|Oonomiya Oogui-shin|ja|大御食神（御食神）|lt=Oonomiya Oogui-shin|lt_ja=大御食神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oonomiya Oogui-shin|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :* {{ill|Oomiya-no-me|ja|大宮女神（大宮売）|lt=Oomiya-no-me|lt_ja=大宮女神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oomiya-no-me|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} :* {{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|ja|事代主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 事代主|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}} :* {{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|阿須波神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuha-no-kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 座摩神|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10928586|lt=Asuha-no-kami|1=Ikasuri no Kami}} :* {{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|坐摩神|lt=Hahiki-no-Kami|lt_ja=波比伎神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 座摩神}} These eight deities, collectively known as the “Serving Table Eight Deities” (御膳八神), were enshrined for the preparation of sacred food by the Daizen-shoku, which differs from the eight deities of the Jingi-kan—four of which are different—in that the latter serve to protect the Emperor <ref name="Matsumae 2016" /><ref name="Matsumae 2016" />. However, since the core deities are common, some scholars speculate that these temporary Daijosai rites represent the original form of the permanent Jingi-kan ceremonies <ref name="Matsumae 2016" />. == Footnotes ==
{{reflist}}
== References == * {{Cite book|language=ja|author=|chapter=|year=|title={{ill|Kokushi Daijiten|ja|国史大辞典|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokushi Daijiten|1=National History Encyclopedia|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6426904}}|publisher={{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|1=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q4346755}}|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference=Masatsugu Kurabayashi, “Yashinden”|ref={{Harvid|Yashinden (National History)}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference=Yoshikazu Suzuki, “Mikanna”|ref={{Harvid|Mikanna (National History)}}}}. * {{Cite book|language=ja|editor=Shikinaisha Kenkyūkai|author=|chapter=|year=1979|title=Shikinaisha Chōsa Hōkoku Vol. 1|publisher={{ill|Kogakkan University|en|Kogakkan University|qid=Q1797584|lt=Kōgakkan University|1=Kogakkan University}} Publishing Department|page=|isbn=|ref=}} ** {{Wikicite|reference=Introduction|ref={{Harvid|Introduction (In-ceremony Shrine)}}}}, {{Wikicite|reference="Mikanna Eight Deities"|ref={{Harvid|Mikanna Worship Deity Eight Shrines (In-ceremony Shrines)}}}}. * {{Cite book|language=ja|editor=|author=Seiji Okada|year=2011|chapter=Ceremonies of the Jingi-kan – The Deities of Saiin and the Service of the Mikanna –|title=New Edition of the Ancient History of Shrines|publisher={{ill|Gakuseisha|ja|学生社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gakuseisha|1=Gakuseisha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11448819}}|isbn=4311203020|ref={{Harvid|Ancient History of Shrines|2011th year }}}}
== Related Literature == * {{ill|Koji Ruien|en|Koji Ruien|qid=Q701081|lt=Kojirui-en|1=Koji Ruien}}, edited by the Shinto Priests’ Office, section on Jingi-kan Deities. ** [{{NDLDC|1873459/446}} {{ill|Kojirui-en Vol. 6|ja|古事類苑 第6冊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kojirui-en Vol. 6|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}] (National Diet Library Digital Collection), see panels 446–450.
